source: GAAP ASC 606 — Revenue from Contracts with Customers
text: ASC 606 requires revenue to be recognized when control of a promised good or service transfers to the customer, in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to be entitled to. The standard prescribes a five-step model: identify the contract, identify the performance obligations, determine the transaction price, allocate the transaction price to each performance obligation, and recognize revenue as each obligation is satisfied. This replaced the previous industry-specific and rules-based revenue guidance with a single principles-based framework applicable across most industries.

The matching principle requires expenses to be recorded in the same period as the revenues they helped generate, regardless of when cash actually changes hands. This is a core tenet of accrual-basis accounting and underlies practices such as depreciating a fixed asset over its useful life rather than expensing its full cost when purchased, or accruing a bonus expense in the period the related work was performed. Proper matching prevents net income from being distorted by the timing of cash receipts and disbursements.

Regulation S-X governs the form and content of financial statements required in registration statements, periodic reports, and other filings submitted to the SEC by public companies. It specifies requirements such as the number of years of financial statements to be presented, rules for pro forma financial information, and qualification and independence standards for auditors. Article 3 of Regulation S-X, for example, sets out general instructions for the balance sheets and income statements of registrants, while other articles address industry-specific disclosures such as those for banks or insurance companies.

The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) sets auditing standards that registered public accounting firms must follow when auditing U.S. public companies. Core standards require auditors to obtain reasonable assurance that financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud, through procedures such as risk assessment, testing of internal controls, and substantive testing of account balances. PCAOB standards also require an integrated audit of internal control over financial reporting for accelerated filers, in addition to the audit of the financial statements themselves.

Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires the CEO and CFO of a public company to personally certify that a periodic report does not contain material misstatements and that they have evaluated the effectiveness of the company's disclosure controls. Section 404 goes further, requiring management to assess and report on the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting (ICFR), and for larger accelerated filers, requiring the external auditor to independently attest to that assessment. Together these sections shifted significant personal accountability for financial reporting accuracy onto senior executives.

IFRS (used in most of the world) and U.S. GAAP differ in several material ways: IFRS permits the revaluation of certain fixed assets and intangibles to fair value, while GAAP generally requires historical cost; IFRS uses a single-step impairment test for long-lived assets, whereas GAAP historically used a two-step test; and inventory accounting under IFRS prohibits the LIFO method, which remains permitted under GAAP. IFRS also tends to be more principles-based with less industry-specific guidance, while GAAP contains extensive detailed, rules-based guidance for specific transaction types and industries.

The statement of cash flows organizes all cash inflows and outflows into three sections: operating activities (cash generated from core business operations, starting from net income and adjusting for non-cash items and working capital changes), investing activities (cash used for or generated by the purchase/sale of long-term assets and investments), and financing activities (cash flows from debt issuance/repayment, equity issuance, and dividends). The sum of the three sections reconciles the change in cash and cash equivalents from the beginning to the end of the reporting period, tying back to the cash balance on the balance sheet.

The debt-to-equity ratio is calculated as total liabilities divided by total shareholders' equity, and it measures the degree to which a company finances its operations through debt versus wholly owned funds. A higher ratio indicates greater reliance on borrowed capital and generally implies higher financial risk and leverage, while a lower ratio suggests a more conservative capital structure. Acceptable ratios vary significantly by industry — capital-intensive sectors like utilities and manufacturing typically carry higher ratios than asset-light sectors like software.

EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) is a non-GAAP metric intended to approximate a company's operating cash-generating ability by stripping out financing decisions, tax jurisdictions, and non-cash accounting charges. It is widely used for comparing profitability across companies with different capital structures or tax situations, and is common in valuation multiples such as EV/EBITDA. Its key limitations are that it ignores capital expenditure requirements, excludes the real cash cost of debt service, and can be manipulated through aggressive add-backs, so it should not be treated as a substitute for cash flow from operations or net income.

An accounts receivable aging report categorizes outstanding customer invoices into time buckets based on how long they have been unpaid — typically current, 1-30 days past due, 31-60, 61-90, and 90+ days past due. This analysis helps a company identify collection risk, estimate an appropriate allowance for doubtful accounts, and prioritize collection efforts on the oldest and largest balances. A rising concentration of receivables in the 90+ day bucket is generally an early warning sign of deteriorating customer credit quality or collections process breakdowns.

Segregation of duties is an internal control principle requiring that no single individual have control over two or more phases of a transaction or operation — typically separating authorization, custody of assets, and record-keeping among different people. For example, the person who approves a vendor payment should not also be the person who can add new vendors to the system or reconcile the bank account. This control reduces the risk of fraud and error by ensuring that a single person could not both perpetrate and conceal a misstatement without collusion.

ASC 820 defines fair value as the price that would be received to sell an asset or paid to transfer a liability in an orderly transaction between market participants at the measurement date, and it establishes a three-level fair value hierarchy. Level 1 inputs are unadjusted quoted prices in active markets for identical assets, Level 2 inputs are observable inputs other than quoted prices (such as quoted prices for similar assets or market-corroborated inputs), and Level 3 inputs are unobservable inputs requiring significant management judgment, such as discounted cash flow assumptions. Entities must disclose which level of the hierarchy each fair-valued item falls into.
